section: 3. Interactive FIR Lowpass for Noise Reduction
text:
3. Interactive FIR Lowpass for Noise Reduction
We now filter the noisy sine using a simple FIR lowpass. Here we use a rectangular‑window sinc lowpass to keep the code short and transparent.
You can:

Adjust cutoff frequency fc
Adjust filter length Nf
See how this affects time‑domain output and spectrum













Tip


Try:

fc = 800 vs fc = 2000
Nf = 31, 51, 201

Observe:

Larger Nf → sharper transition, but more delay and more start‑up transient.
Smaller fc → more aggressive low‑frequency focus (but may attenuate the 500 Hz tone).

6. Reactive SNR Experiment – Filtering Noisy Sine
Let’s connect filter design to SNR improvement.
Use sliders to:

title: 7.2 FIR Filter Design – Applications & Advanced Methods
text:
7. Frequency Sampling Method – Direct Experiment
Now we build a tiny frequency sampling designer for a low‑order FIR.
You specify magnitudes \(H_k\) at discrete frequencies, and we compute \(h(n)\) and its frequency response.
Define Sampled Magnitudes

viewof H0 = Inputs.range([0, 1], {step: 0.1, value: 1, label: "H0"})
viewof H1 = Inputs.range([0, 1], {step: 0.1, value: 1, label: "H1"})
viewof H2 = Inputs.range([0, 1], {step: 0.1, value: 0, label: "H2"})
viewof H3 = Inputs.range([0, 1], {step: 0.1, value: 0, label: "H3"})





























We’ll design a 7‑tap (\(N=7\)) linear‑phase FIR (as in Example 7.12) using:

\(H_0 = \text{H0}\)
\(H_1 = \text{H1}\)
\(H_2 = \text{H2}\)
\(H_3 = \text{H3}\)













Tip


Set \((H_0,H_1,H_2,H_3)=(1,1,0,0)\) to recover Example 7.12. Then, try gradual transitions like \((1,0.7,0.3,0)\) to see how the response changes.

title: 7.2 FIR Filter Design – Applications & Advanced Methods
section: 8. Gibbs vs Smooth Transition – Frequency Sampling
text:
8. Gibbs vs Smooth Transition – Frequency Sampling
We now create a tiny comparison like Example 7.13/7.14:

Abrupt spec → strong ripple
Smooth spec → reduced ripple


viewof H_mid = Inputs.range([0, 1], {
  step: 0.1,
  value: 0,
  label: "Middle transition magnitude H_mid (0 = sharp, 0.5 = smooth)"
})












Try H_mid = 0 (abrupt) vs H_mid = 0.5 for smoother transition.

title: 7.2 FIR Filter Design – Applications & Advanced Methods
text:
9. Parks–McClellan Intuition – Weighting Trade‑Off
We can’t implement full Remez here easily, but we can experiment with weights in a very simple polynomial approximation problem as an analogy.
We approximate a piecewise constant function \(d(\omega)\) with a degree‑1 polynomial \(p(\omega)=a\omega + b\), using different weights in two regions.

viewof W_pass = Inputs.range([0.1, 10], {
  step: 0.1,
  value: 1,
  label: "Weight in passband (W_pass)"
})
viewof W_stop = Inputs.range([0.1, 10], {
  step: 0.1,
  value: 5,
  label: "Weight in stopband (W_stop)"
})



























Note


This is an analogy: in full Parks–McClellan, \(W_p\) and \(W_s\) control relative error in passband vs stopband. Increasing \(W_s\) forces the algorithm to “work harder” in the stopband and accept more passband ripple, and vice versa.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Capstone Design Course Introduction
From Needs to Validated Systems using the V‑Model & ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148
Document Context: DCP‑000 Course Guide, Rev 1.0

This deck is the first session introduction for the Capstone Design course. Goal: give students a big‑picture roadmap of the course, explain why we use the V‑Model and 29148, and how the DCP document series and traceability tie everything together. Remind them: this is not “just a project course”; it is a systems engineering lab that simulates industry practice.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Session Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain the overall V‑Model lifecycle used in the course.
Describe the nine stages of the Capstone project and their main deliverables.
Explain the difference between verification and validation.
Use the basic ID and traceability scheme (STR, REQ, CMP, IMP, TST, VER, VAL).
Understand the DCP document package and gate reviews.


Emphasize that this lecture is meta‑level: today is about how the course works, not technical content. Students should leave understanding what success looks like administratively and methodologically.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Why a Structured Capstone?


Engineering reality:

Real systems are complex (HW + SW + users + environment).
Failing late is expensive (money, safety, reputation).
Industry uses process models (V‑Model, 29148, 15288) to reduce risk.

Course philosophy:

Your capstone is a mini‑industry project, not just a demo.
You will practice the full lifecycle from need → requirements → design → code/hardware → tests → handover.
We grade both technical quality and engineering discipline.


Real‑world ECE examples:

Medical device firmware must trace every requirement to a test (FDA).
Automotive ECUs follow V‑Model (ISO 26262) to prove safety.
Telecom base stations use detailed requirements / architecture for complex RF + DSP chains.







Important


Key message: You’re learning how real engineering organizations work.





Use real anecdotes if you have them (e.g., a product recall from a requirement misunderstanding). Point out that many students will interview at companies that ask about requirements, testing, traceability, not just coding syntax or basic circuits.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Standards Backbone for the Course


Core standards guiding this course:

ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering
ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288:2023 System life cycle processes — provides the V‑Model context.

We map the course to these by:

Structuring stages according to 29148 processes.
Using the V‑Model as the project roadmap.
Emphasizing requirements quality, verification, validation, and traceability.















Clarify that students are not expected to memorize the standard numbers, but they should understand the concepts those standards enforce: - Good requirements. - Systematic verification/validation. - Lifecycle thinking.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Core Principles of the Capstone

Requirements before solutions

Understand the problem and needs before jumping to circuits, code, or schematics.

Traceability always

Every artifact (requirement, design element, test) is linked up and down.
The Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) is your single source of truth.

Verifiable & Validated

Verification: “Did we build it right?”
Validation: “Did we build the right thing?”








Tip


Think of requirements like a contract: You can’t claim you met the contract unless each clause is testable and tested.




This slide defines the mental model for the whole course. Use a simple story (e.g., building a smart thermostat): - Requirement: “User can set temperature between 16–30°C with ±0.5°C accuracy.” - Verification: bench‑test the temp sensor and control algorithm. - Validation: check if actual users are comfortable and find the UI usable.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
The V‑Model – Big Picture

















Note


Left side: Define & design. Right side: Test & prove. Bottom: Implementation.




Point out the mirror relationships:

Stakeholder needs ↔︎ Stakeholder validation.
System requirements ↔︎ System verification.
Component design ↔︎ Unit tests.

Students will live this structure through the nine stages.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Course Stages Overview
We implement the V‑Model in nine stages:

Project Framing & Concept of Operations (ConOps)
Stakeholder Requirements
System Requirements
Architecture & Component Design
Implementation
Unit / Component Verification
System / Integration Verification
Stakeholder Validation
Project Finalization & Handover

Each stage:

Has a clear purpose.
Produces a DCP document.
Is gated by a review before moving on.


Tell students: “Think of this like levels in a game. You can’t move to Level 3 until you beat the boss at Level 2 (gate review).” This helps them anticipate workload and milestones across the year.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Traceability: The Backbone of the Project
text:
Traceability: The Backbone of the Project


Artifacts and IDs:

Stakeholder Requirement → STR-SY-### or STR-SW-###
System Requirement → REQ-SY-### or REQ-SW-###
Component → CMP-SY-### or CMP-SW-###
Implementation Artifact → IMP-SY-### or IMP-SW-###
Unit Test → TST-SY-### or TST-SW-###
System Verification Test → VER-SY-### or VER-SW-###
Validation Activity → VAL-SY-### or VAL-SW-###

All of these live in the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM).














Explain vertical traceability: STR → REQ → CMP → IMP → TST → VER → VAL. Mention horizontal traceability: - Stage 2 ↔︎ Stage 8 (STR ↔︎ VAL) - Stage 3 ↔︎ Stage 7 (REQ ↔︎ VER) - Stage 4 ↔︎ Stage 6 (CMP ↔︎ TST)
Students will maintain this in a spreadsheet or requirements tool.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Traceability in the V‑Model

















Note


Horizontal links: - STR-* ↔︎ VAL-* (Stakeholder needs ↔︎ Acceptance tests) - REQ-* ↔︎ VER-* (System requirements ↔︎ System tests) - CMP-* ↔︎ TST-* (Component design ↔︎ Unit tests)




This is the “V‑Model as a graph” where nodes are artifacts and edges are traceability links. Use an example: - STR-SY-001: “Lab tech can measure temperature remotely.” - REQ-SY-003: “System shall transmit temperature updates every 1 s with ±0.5°C accuracy.” - CMP-SY-010: “Wireless module & protocol design.” - TST-SY-007: “Unit test for wireless throughput and reliability.” - VER-SY-002: “System‑level test of telemetry performance.” - VAL-SY-001: “User acceptance test in actual lab environment.”

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations


V‑Model Level: Top‑left – Concept / Need
29148 Processes:

Business or Mission Analysis
Stakeholder Identification

Capstone Activities:

Define the problem domain and business/mission need.
Analyze existing solutions and their limitations.
Characterize environment: users, constraints, stakeholders.
Select a solution class (not a detailed design).
Produce a Concept of Operations (ConOps).


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑100A Project Proposal

Problem statement
Stakeholder map
Operational scenarios
High‑level success criteria








Tip


Focus on the “why”, not on schematics or specific chips yet.





Warn students against “premature design fixation”.
If someone says, “We’re building with Raspberry Pi and LoRa,” ask:

What problem are you solving?
Who are your stakeholders?
What are the operational scenarios?

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 2 – Stakeholder Needs & Requirements


V‑Model Level: Upper‑left – Stakeholder / User Requirements
29148 Processes:

Stakeholder Needs & Requirements Definition
Early Requirements Validation (planning)

Capstone Activities:

Identify all stakeholders: advisors, clients, end‑users, regulators, lab staff.
Conduct interviews, surveys, observations.
Use use cases, user stories, and scenarios to elicit needs.
Prioritize needs (e.g., MoSCoW, Kano).
Resolve conflicts and negotiate scope.
Develop a Stakeholder Requirements Specification (StRS).


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑100B Stakeholder Requirements

STR-* IDs
High‑level acceptance criteria
Initial Validation Plan (VAL-* planned)


Quality Criteria (29148):

Necessary
Unambiguous
Complete (to agreed scope)
Traceable to business needs



Use a quick in‑class exercise: Give them a vague requirement like “System should be easy to use” and ask how to make it measurable and testable (e.g., “User shall complete basic task X within 2 minutes without external help”).

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements


V‑Model Level: Mid‑left – System Requirements
29148 Processes:

System/Software Requirements Definition
Requirements Analysis

Capstone Activities:

Transform stakeholder needs (StRS) into technical requirements.
Categorize:

Functional
Performance
Interface
Security
Usability
Environmental / Reliability / Safety

Define acceptance criteria and verification methods:

Test, Analysis, Model, Demonstration, Inspection (TAMDI).

Ensure requirements are:

Verifiable, Feasible, Singular (one requirement per statement).

Produce SyRS/SRS.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑200 System Requirements

REQ-* IDs
Bidirectional traceability: STR-* → REQ-*
Initial Verification Plan (VER-* planned)


Example requirement form:
\[
\text{REQ-SY-023: The system shall}~ <\text{observable behavior}> \\
\text{under}~ <\text{conditions}> ~\text{with}~ <\text{quantitative criteria}>.
\]


Clarify the difference between StRS and SyRS/SRS:

StRS: stakeholder language (“The lab wants to …”).
SyRS: engineering language, quantitative, testable.

Use a simple example:

STR: “Student should be able to log in quickly.”
REQ: “System shall authenticate a user within 2 s in 95% of attempts over campus Wi‑Fi.”

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 4 – Architecture & Component Design


V‑Model Level: Mid‑left – Architecture and Detailed Design
29148 / 15288 Role:

Architecture Definition
Design Definition
Requirements Allocation

Capstone Activities:

Develop system architecture:

Block diagrams
Data flow, control flow, or state diagrams

Allocate each REQ-* to one or more components (CMP-*).
Define interfaces (signals, protocols, voltages, APIs).
Develop detailed design:

Algorithms, data structures (SW)
Schematics, PCB layouts (HW)

Update RTM: REQ-* → CMP-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑300 Design

Architecture views
Component specifications (CMP-*)
Requirements Allocation Matrix
Initial Test Plan (TST-* planned)




















Important


Every component should exist because it satisfies at least one requirement. If you can’t map a component to a requirement, question why it exists.




This is where many projects either stay coherent or become a “bag of parts.” Highlight interface definitions as common failure points in integration.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 5 – Implementation / Construction


V‑Model Level: Bottom – Coding / Building
29148 / 15288 Role:

Implementation Process
Configuration Management

Capstone Activities:

Implement the design:

Develop software modules (embedded, apps, services).
Build hardware (prototypes, PCBs, mechanical assemblies).

Use version control (Git) and branching strategies.
Perform code / design reviews against requirements.
Manage change requests when implementation forces requirement/design updates.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑400 Implementation

Implementation artifacts (IMP-*)
Build procedures
Updated requirements baseline & change log








Note


Expect iterative build–test–fix cycles.
The key is to manage changes and keep traceability up to date.





Make clear that this is the most “hands‑on” stage, but good work here depends on good requirements and design upstream.
Use a quick example of a Git log entry referencing a requirement ID (REQ-SW-012) in the commit message as a traceability practice.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 6 – Unit / Component Verification


V‑Model Level: Lower‑right – Unit Testing / Component Verification
29148 Process: Verification at component level
Capstone Activities:

Design and execute unit tests for each component (CMP-*).
Map each test (TST-*) to:

Component(s)
Allocated requirements (REQ-*)

Record test procedures, environments, and results.
Log defects and perform root‑cause analysis.
Update RTM: CMP-* → TST-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500A Unit Verification

TST-* test cases
Unit test logs
Defect reports








Tip


For every test case, be able to answer:

“Which requirement does this verify?”






For ECE:

Unit test for a DSP filter module: check frequency response vs spec.
Unit test for a sensor board: measure offset, noise, linearity vs REQ-SY-0xx. You can show simple pseudo‑code or a testbench listing referencing requirement IDs in comments.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 7 – System / Integration Verification


V‑Model Level: Mid‑right – Integration & System Test
29148 Process: Verification against system requirements
Capstone Activities:

Integrate components incrementally (e.g., bottom‑up, top‑down, or mixed).
Test interfaces and interactions (timing, protocols, data consistency).
Execute system test procedures derived from SyRS/SRS.
Ensure full coverage of REQ-* in system verification.
Update RTM: REQ-* ↔︎ VER-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500B System Verification

VER-* system test cases and results
Integration & system test reports
Updated RTM with coverage metrics








Important


Verification = “Did we build it right?” Does the integrated system meet all system requirements?





Highlight emergent behavior: problems that only appear when subsystems interact. Examples in ECE: RF interference when two subsystems are close, timing issues between microcontroller and peripheral, race conditions in distributed systems, etc.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 8 – Stakeholder Validation & Acceptance


V‑Model Level: Top‑right – System / Acceptance Testing
29148 Process: Validation
Capstone Activities:

Execute system‑level tests against stakeholder needs (StRS).
Conduct acceptance testing with real stakeholders.
Observe users in realistic environments or scenarios.
Ask:


“Does this system solve the original business/mission problem?”


Gather feedback, capture issues or change requests.
Produce Validation Report and Acceptance Sign‑off.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500C Stakeholder Validation

VAL-* validation activities and results
Stakeholder demo summary
Lessons learned from usage








Important


Validation = “Did we build the right thing?” Even a perfectly verified system can fail validation if it doesn’t meet real needs.





Use a simple story: A system that meets every technical spec but has a terrible UI and users refuse to adopt it → verification succeeded, validation failed. Encourage teams to involve stakeholders early and often (e.g., quick UI mockup sessions).

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 9 – Project Finalization & Handover


V‑Model Level: Spans entire V – Transition & Closure
29148 / 15288 Processes:

Information Management
Transition
Disposal Planning (at least conceptually)

Capstone Activities:

Finalize all documentation:

StRS, SyRS/SRS
Design docs
Test reports
User & maintenance manuals

Freeze and archive the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM).
Conduct a project post‑mortem (lessons learned).
Present final system to faculty, sponsors, and stakeholders.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑600 Final Report & Handover

Complete RTM
Project archive (source, CAD, configs)
Handover instructions








Note


Think like a professional engineer handing a system to a client or another engineering team.





Ask: “If you left the company tomorrow, could someone else maintain or extend your system using only your final package?” Use this to motivate clean documentation, code comments, and design rationale.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Example: Smart Lab Sensor Network (ECE Context)
text:
Example: Smart Lab Sensor Network (ECE Context)


Concept:
Design a wireless sensor network that monitors temperature and humidity in teaching labs and pushes data to a dashboard.
Sample Stakeholder Requirements (STR-*):

STR-SY-001: Lab technicians shall monitor each lab’s temperature in real time from a central dashboard.
STR-SY-002: System shall alert staff when temperature in any lab exceeds 28°C for more than 10 minutes.


Derived System Requirements (REQ-*):

REQ-SY-010: Each sensor node shall measure temperature from 10–40°C with ±0.5°C accuracy.
REQ-SY-011: Each node shall transmit measurements at least once every 60 s.
REQ-SW-020: The server software shall generate an alert within 2 min of detecting a lab exceeding 28°C for 10 min.

Potential Tests (TST-*, VER-*, VAL-*):

TST-SY-001: Bench‑test temperature sensor calibration.
VER-SW-002: System‑level test of data latency and alert generation.
VAL-SY-001: Validation session with lab staff to confirm alerts are useful and understandable.



Walk through this example across the stages:

Stage 1: Why monitor labs? Who cares?
Stage 2: What do technicians, facilities, and students need?
Stage 3: Convert those into quantitative specs.
Stage 4: Architecture (nodes, gateways, server, dashboard).
Stage 5: HW + SW implementation.
Stage 6–7: Testing sensors, network, alerts.
Stage 8: Let real lab staff try the system and collect feedback.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Summary – What You Should Take Away Today
text:
Summary – What You Should Take Away Today

The course uses the V‑Model and ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 to structure your capstone.
You will work through nine stages from problem framing to handover.
Traceability is central: STR-* → REQ-* → CMP-* → IMP-* → TST-* → VER-* → VAL-*.
DCP documents (DCP‑100A … DCP‑600) are your official artifacts for gate reviews.
Verification asks: “Did we build it right?” Validation asks: “Did we build the right thing?”







Important


Your success is not just the final demo. It is demonstrating that you can engineer a system professionally, from needs to validated solution.




Close by connecting this to students’ future careers and accreditation outcomes (ABET, etc., if applicable). Invite questions specifically about: - Scope of projects - Team composition - Tools for RTM (spreadsheets, requirements plugins, etc.)

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Capstone Design Course Introduction
From Needs to Validated Systems using the V‑Model & ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148
Document Context: DCP‑000 Course Guide, Rev 1.0

This deck is the first session introduction for the Capstone Design course. Goal: give students a big‑picture roadmap of the course, explain why we use the V‑Model and 29148, and how the DCP document series and traceability tie everything together. Remind them: this is not “just a project course”; it is a systems engineering lab that simulates industry practice.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Session Learning Objectives
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain the overall V‑Model lifecycle used in the course.
Describe the nine stages of the Capstone project and their main deliverables.
Explain the difference between verification and validation.
Use the basic ID and traceability scheme (STR, REQ, CMP, IMP, TST, VER, VAL).
Understand the DCP document package and gate reviews.


Emphasize that this lecture is meta‑level: today is about how the course works, not technical content. Students should leave understanding what success looks like administratively and methodologically.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Why a Structured Capstone?


Engineering reality:

Real systems are complex (HW + SW + users + environment).
Failing late is expensive (money, safety, reputation).
Industry uses process models (V‑Model, 29148, 15288) to reduce risk.

Course philosophy:

Your capstone is a mini‑industry project, not just a demo.
You will practice the full lifecycle from need → requirements → design → code/hardware → tests → handover.
We grade both technical quality and engineering discipline.


Real‑world ECE examples:

Medical device firmware must trace every requirement to a test (FDA).
Automotive ECUs follow V‑Model (ISO 26262) to prove safety.
Telecom base stations use detailed requirements / architecture for complex RF + DSP chains.







Important


Key message: You’re learning how real engineering organizations work.





Use real anecdotes if you have them (e.g., a product recall from a requirement misunderstanding). Point out that many students will interview at companies that ask about requirements, testing, traceability, not just coding syntax or basic circuits.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Standards Backbone for the Course


Core standards guiding this course:

ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 Life cycle processes — Requirements engineering
ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288:2023 System life cycle processes — provides the V‑Model context.

We map the course to these by:

Structuring stages according to 29148 processes.
Using the V‑Model as the project roadmap.
Emphasizing requirements quality, verification, validation, and traceability.















Clarify that students are not expected to memorize the standard numbers, but they should understand the concepts those standards enforce: - Good requirements. - Systematic verification/validation. - Lifecycle thinking.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Core Principles of the Capstone

Requirements before solutions

Understand the problem and needs before jumping to circuits, code, or schematics.

Traceability always

Every artifact (requirement, design element, test) is linked up and down.
The Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM) is your single source of truth.

Verifiable & Validated

Verification: “Did we build it right?”
Validation: “Did we build the right thing?”








Tip


Think of requirements like a contract: You can’t claim you met the contract unless each clause is testable and tested.




This slide defines the mental model for the whole course. Use a simple story (e.g., building a smart thermostat): - Requirement: “User can set temperature between 16–30°C with ±0.5°C accuracy.” - Verification: bench‑test the temp sensor and control algorithm. - Validation: check if actual users are comfortable and find the UI usable.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
The V‑Model – Big Picture

















Note


Left side: Define & design.
Right side: Test & prove.
Bottom: Implementation.




Point out the mirror relationships:

Stakeholder needs ↔︎ Stakeholder validation.
System requirements ↔︎ System verification.
Component design ↔︎ Unit tests.

Students will live this structure through the nine stages.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Course Stages Overview
We implement the V‑Model in nine stages:

Project Framing & Concept of Operations (ConOps)
Stakeholder Requirements
System Requirements
Architecture & Component Design
Implementation
Unit / Component Verification
System / Integration Verification
Stakeholder Validation
Project Finalization & Handover

Each stage:

Has a clear purpose.
Produces a DCP document.
Is gated by a review before moving on.


Tell students: “Think of this like levels in a game. You can’t move to Level 3 until you beat the boss at Level 2 (gate review).” This helps them anticipate workload and milestones across the year.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Traceability: The Backbone of the Project
text:
Traceability: The Backbone of the Project


Artifacts and IDs:

Stakeholder Requirement → STR-SY-### or STR-SW-###
System Requirement → REQ-SY-### or REQ-SW-###
Component → CMP-SY-### or CMP-SW-###
Implementation Artifact → IMP-SY-### or IMP-SW-###
Unit Test → TST-SY-### or TST-SW-###
System Verification Test → VER-SY-### or VER-SW-###
Validation Activity → VAL-SY-### or VAL-SW-###

All of these live in the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM).














Explain vertical traceability: STR → REQ → CMP → IMP → TST → VER → VAL. Mention horizontal traceability: - Stage 2 ↔︎ Stage 8 (STR ↔︎ VAL) - Stage 3 ↔︎ Stage 7 (REQ ↔︎ VER) - Stage 4 ↔︎ Stage 6 (CMP ↔︎ TST)
Students will maintain this in a spreadsheet or requirements tool.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Traceability in the V‑Model

















Note


Horizontal links:

STR-* ↔︎ VAL-* (Stakeholder needs ↔︎ Acceptance tests)
REQ-* ↔︎ VER-* (System requirements ↔︎ System tests)
CMP-* ↔︎ TST-* (Component design ↔︎ Unit tests)





This is the “V‑Model as a graph” where nodes are artifacts and edges are traceability links. Use an example: - STR-SY-001: “Lab tech can measure temperature remotely.” - REQ-SY-003: “System shall transmit temperature updates every 1 s with ±0.5°C accuracy.” - CMP-SY-010: “Wireless module & protocol design.” - TST-SY-007: “Unit test for wireless throughput and reliability.” - VER-SY-002: “System‑level test of telemetry performance.” - VAL-SY-001: “User acceptance test in actual lab environment.”

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations
text:
Stage 1 – Project Framing & Concept of Operations


V‑Model Level: Top‑left – Concept / Need
29148 Processes:

Business or Mission Analysis
Stakeholder Identification

Capstone Activities:

Define the problem domain and business/mission need.
Analyze existing solutions and their limitations.
Characterize environment: users, constraints, stakeholders.
Select a solution class (not a detailed design).
Produce a Concept of Operations (ConOps).


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑100A Project Proposal

Problem statement
Stakeholder map
Operational scenarios
High‑level success criteria








Tip


Focus on the “why”, not on schematics or specific chips yet.





Warn students against “premature design fixation”.
If someone says, “We’re building with Raspberry Pi and LoRa,” ask:

What problem are you solving?
Who are your stakeholders?
What are the operational scenarios?

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 2 – Stakeholder Needs & Requirements


V‑Model Level: Upper‑left – Stakeholder / User Requirements
29148 Processes:

Stakeholder Needs & Requirements Definition
Early Requirements Validation (planning)

Capstone Activities:

Identify all stakeholders: advisors, clients, end‑users, regulators, lab staff.
Conduct interviews, surveys, observations.
Use use cases, user stories, and scenarios to elicit needs.
Prioritize needs (e.g., MoSCoW, Kano).
Resolve conflicts and negotiate scope.
Develop a Stakeholder Requirements Specification (StRS).


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑100B Stakeholder Requirements

STR-* IDs
High‑level acceptance criteria
Initial Validation Plan (VAL-* planned)


Quality Criteria (29148):

Necessary
Unambiguous
Complete (to agreed scope)
Traceable to business needs



Use a quick in‑class exercise: Give them a vague requirement like “System should be easy to use” and ask how to make it measurable and testable (e.g., “User shall complete basic task X within 2 minutes without external help”).

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 3 – System / Software Requirements


V‑Model Level: Mid‑left – System Requirements
29148 Processes:

System/Software Requirements Definition
Requirements Analysis

Capstone Activities:

Transform stakeholder needs (StRS) into technical requirements.
Categorize:

Functional
Performance
Interface
Security
Usability
Environmental / Reliability / Safety

Define acceptance criteria and verification methods:

Test, Analysis, Model, Demonstration, Inspection (TAMDI).

Ensure requirements are:

Verifiable, Feasible, Singular (one requirement per statement).

Produce SyRS/SRS.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑200 System Requirements

REQ-* IDs
Bidirectional traceability: STR-* → REQ-*
Initial Verification Plan (VER-* planned)


Example requirement form:
\[
\text{REQ-SY-023: The system shall}~ <\text{observable behavior}> \\
\text{under}~ <\text{conditions}> ~\text{with}~ <\text{quantitative criteria}>.
\]


Clarify the difference between StRS and SyRS/SRS:

StRS: stakeholder language (“The lab wants to …”).
SyRS: engineering language, quantitative, testable.

Use a simple example:

STR: “Student should be able to log in quickly.”
REQ: “System shall authenticate a user within 2 s in 95% of attempts over campus Wi‑Fi.”

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 4 – Architecture & Component Design


V‑Model Level: Mid‑left – Architecture and Detailed Design
29148 / 15288 Role:

Architecture Definition
Design Definition
Requirements Allocation

Capstone Activities:

Develop system architecture:

Block diagrams
Data flow, control flow, or state diagrams

Allocate each REQ-* to one or more components (CMP-*).
Define interfaces (signals, protocols, voltages, APIs).
Develop detailed design:

Algorithms, data structures (SW)
Schematics, PCB layouts (HW)

Update RTM: REQ-* → CMP-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑300 Design

Architecture views
Component specifications (CMP-*)
Requirements Allocation Matrix
Initial Test Plan (TST-* planned)




















Important


Every component should exist because it satisfies at least one requirement. If you can’t map a component to a requirement, question why it exists.




This is where many projects either stay coherent or become a “bag of parts.” Highlight interface definitions as common failure points in integration.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 5 – Implementation / Construction


V‑Model Level: Bottom – Coding / Building
29148 / 15288 Role:

Implementation Process
Configuration Management

Capstone Activities:

Implement the design:

Develop software modules (embedded, apps, services).
Build hardware (prototypes, PCBs, mechanical assemblies).

Use version control (Git) and branching strategies.
Perform code / design reviews against requirements.
Manage change requests when implementation forces requirement/design updates.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑400 Implementation

Implementation artifacts (IMP-*)
Build procedures
Updated requirements baseline & change log








Note


Expect iterative build–test–fix cycles.
The key is to manage changes and keep traceability up to date.





Make clear that this is the most “hands‑on” stage, but good work here depends on good requirements and design upstream.
Use a quick example of a Git log entry referencing a requirement ID (REQ-SW-012) in the commit message as a traceability practice.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 6 – Unit / Component Verification


V‑Model Level: Lower‑right – Unit Testing / Component Verification
29148 Process: Verification at component level
Capstone Activities:

Design and execute unit tests for each component (CMP-*).
Map each test (TST-*) to:

Component(s)
Allocated requirements (REQ-*)

Record test procedures, environments, and results.
Log defects and perform root‑cause analysis.
Update RTM: CMP-* → TST-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500A Unit Verification

TST-* test cases
Unit test logs
Defect reports








Tip


For every test case, be able to answer:

“Which requirement does this verify?”






For ECE:

Unit test for a DSP filter module: check frequency response vs spec.
Unit test for a sensor board: measure offset, noise, linearity vs REQ-SY-0xx. You can show simple pseudo‑code or a testbench listing referencing requirement IDs in comments.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 7 – System / Integration Verification


V‑Model Level: Mid‑right – Integration & System Test
29148 Process: Verification against system requirements
Capstone Activities:

Integrate components incrementally (e.g., bottom‑up, top‑down, or mixed).
Test interfaces and interactions (timing, protocols, data consistency).
Execute system test procedures derived from SyRS/SRS.
Ensure full coverage of REQ-* in system verification.
Update RTM: REQ-* ↔︎ VER-*.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500B System Verification

VER-* system test cases and results
Integration & system test reports
Updated RTM with coverage metrics








Important


Verification = “Did we build it right?” Does the integrated system meet all system requirements?





Highlight emergent behavior: problems that only appear when subsystems interact. Examples in ECE: RF interference when two subsystems are close, timing issues between microcontroller and peripheral, race conditions in distributed systems, etc.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 8 – Stakeholder Validation & Acceptance


V‑Model Level: Top‑right – System / Acceptance Testing
29148 Process: Validation
Capstone Activities:

Execute system‑level tests against stakeholder needs (StRS).
Conduct acceptance testing with real stakeholders.
Observe users in realistic environments or scenarios.
Ask:


“Does this system solve the original business/mission problem?”


Gather feedback, capture issues or change requests.
Produce Validation Report and Acceptance Sign‑off.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑500C Stakeholder Validation

VAL-* validation activities and results
Stakeholder demo summary
Lessons learned from usage








Important


Validation = “Did we build the right thing?” Even a perfectly verified system can fail validation if it doesn’t meet real needs.





Use a simple story: A system that meets every technical spec but has a terrible UI and users refuse to adopt it → verification succeeded, validation failed. Encourage teams to involve stakeholders early and often (e.g., quick UI mockup sessions).

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
text:
Stage 9 – Project Finalization & Handover


V‑Model Level: Spans entire V – Transition & Closure
29148 / 15288 Processes:

Information Management
Transition
Disposal Planning (at least conceptually)

Capstone Activities:

Finalize all documentation:

StRS, SyRS/SRS
Design docs
Test reports
User & maintenance manuals

Freeze and archive the Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM).
Conduct a project post‑mortem (lessons learned).
Present final system to faculty, sponsors, and stakeholders.


Key Deliverable:

DCP‑600 Final Report & Handover

Complete RTM
Project archive (source, CAD, configs)
Handover instructions








Note


Think like a professional engineer handing a system to a client or another engineering team.





Ask: “If you left the company tomorrow, could someone else maintain or extend your system using only your final package?” Use this to motivate clean documentation, code comments, and design rationale.

title: Capstone Design Course Introduction
section: Summary – What You Should Take Away Today
text:
Summary – What You Should Take Away Today

The course uses the V‑Model and ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 to structure your capstone.
You will work through nine stages from problem framing to handover.
Traceability is central: STR-* → REQ-* → CMP-* → IMP-* → TST-* → VER-* → VAL-*.
DCP documents (DCP‑100A … DCP‑600) are your official artifacts for gate reviews.
Verification asks: “Did we build it right?” Validation asks: “Did we build the right thing?”







Important


Your success is not just the final demo. It is demonstrating that you can engineer a system professionally, from needs to validated solution.




Close by connecting this to students’ future careers and accreditation outcomes (ABET, etc., if applicable). Invite questions specifically about: - Scope of projects - Team composition - Tools for RTM (spreadsheets, requirements plugins, etc.)

title: Stage 5
text:
Stage 5 – Implementation & Configuration Management
Config Control, Baselines, Change Management, Technical Debt, Integration Planning
Capstone Design – Electrical & Computer Engineering

This session zooms in on Stage 5 of the capstone: Implementation. Students are likely already writing code and building hardware; now we teach them to manage that work professionally using configuration management, baselines, change control, and integration planning. Emphasize: lots of student capstones “die” here due to unmanaged changes and chaotic integration.

title: Stage 5
text:
Learning Objectives – Stage 5
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain what configuration management (CM) means in a capstone project.
Define and use baselines (requirements, design, implementation) in your project.
Apply a simple change control process using change requests and approvals.
Recognize and manage technical debt in both HW and SW.
Plan for integration using a V‑Model mindset.
Relate these concepts to real ECE project examples (embedded systems, RF, power, FPGA, etc.).


These are behavior‑level objectives: students should do CM, not just know definitions. At the end, you can revisit these objectives and ask students to self‑assess where they feel strong/weak.

title: Stage 5
section: Where Are We in the V‑Model?
text:
Where Are We in the V‑Model?

















Note


Stage 5 sits at the bottom of the V:
You turn your designs (DCP‑300) into real artifacts (DCP‑400) while maintaining traceability.




Remind them: - Up to now: mostly documents, diagrams, and planning. - Now: code, hardware, prototypes – but you can’t abandon discipline. Stage 5 sets up the success of Stages 6–8 (testing & validation).

title: Stage 5
text:
Stage 5 in the Course Deliverables


Stage 5 Key Outcomes:

Implementation of planned components (CMP-* → IMP-*).
All work under configuration management (Git, etc.).
Defined implementation baseline at key milestones.
Active change control: every major divergence from design/requirements tracked.
Updated Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM).


DCP‑400 – Implementation
Must include:

List of implementation artifacts (IMP-* IDs for code, schematics, PCBs, etc.).
Build scripts / README for building SW/HW.
Change log (approved changes vs original plan).
Updated baselines and CM strategy (branches/tags).



Stress: DCP‑400 is more than “we built it.” It documents how it was built, what versions are official, and how those versions relate back to requirements and design.

title: Stage 5
text:
Configuration Management – What & Why


Configuration Management (CM) is the discipline of:

Identifying the configuration items (CIs) that define your system.
Controlling changes to those items.
Keeping an accurate record of what version is used where.

Typical CIs in ECE Capstones:

Source code, HDL (VHDL/Verilog), firmware.
Schematics, PCB layouts, BOMs.
FPGA bitstreams, compiled binaries.
Test scripts, calibration data, FPGA constraints files.
Key documents (StRS, SyRS, design, test plans).



















Important


Without CM, you don’t know which version was tested or delivered.





Use the classic horror story:

Team tests version A.
Team demos accidentally with version B that was only half‑tested. They can’t reproduce results because they never tagged the working commit or documented differences.

title: Stage 5
section: Concrete CM Example – FPGA‑Based DSP System
text:
Concrete CM Example – FPGA‑Based DSP System
ECE Example: An FPGA‑based OFDM transceiver for a custom wireless link.
Configuration Items (CIs):

IMP-SW-101: FPGA HDL source (filters, OFDM mod/demod).
IMP-SW-102: MATLAB/NumPy models for algorithm verification.
IMP-SY-201: RF front‑end schematic and PCB layout.
IMP-SY-202: Clock distribution network design and constraints file.
IMP-SW-103: RTL testbenches and simulation scripts.

CM Actions:

All HDL and models in a Git repository.
Major milestones tagged: v0.1-prototype, v1.0-lab-demo.
PCB design files archived with version numbers and date.
Change requests logged when modifying OFDM parameters (e.g., subcarrier spacing).


Tie this back to traceability: - Each IMP-* artifact links back to CMP-* (e.g., OFDM core component) and REQ-* (e.g., throughput requirement). Emphasize the importance of tagging bitstreams and matching them to the Git revision that produced them.

title: Stage 5
text:
Baselines – Freezing a Moving Target


A baseline is a formally agreed version of one or more artifacts that serves as a reference for:

Further development
Testing
Change control

Common baselines in your capstone:

Requirements Baseline – DCP‑200 approved (no change without CR).
Design Baseline – DCP‑300 approved (architecture/components frozen).
Implementation Baseline – DCP‑400 milestone (code/hardware version used for tests).

Once a baseline is set:

Changes must go through a controlled process.



















Note


Baselines reduce chaos: Everyone knows which version is “the official one” at each stage.





Explain that a “baseline” does not mean “can never change.” It means: from now on, changes are intentional, documented, and reviewed, not accidental.

title: Stage 5
section: Change Control – Keeping Changes Under Control
text:
Change Control – Keeping Changes Under Control


Why change control?

New insights: requirements were incomplete or wrong.
Hardware constraints: chosen component is EOL or behaves differently.
Performance issues: design doesn’t meet timing, power, or noise specs.

Minimal Change Control Process for Capstone:

Identify needed change (bug, new requirement, design fix).
Log change request (CR) with:

Description
Reason / impact
Affected IDs (REQ-*, CMP-*, IMP-*, etc.).

Assess impact (performance, schedule, risk).
Decide (approve / reject / defer) as a team (with advisor input).
Implement and update RTM + docs.



















Warning


If you change HW/SW or requirements without a CR, your traceability and test results become invalid.





Show a simple CR template in your LMS or repo (spreadsheet, GitHub Issue template). Encourage linking to related Git commits and requirement IDs.

title: Stage 5
section: ECE Change Control Example – Embedded Motor Controller
text:
ECE Change Control Example – Embedded Motor Controller
Project: BLDC motor controller for a robotics platform.

Requirement:

REQ-SY-030: “Controller shall regulate motor speed within ±5% of setpoint under loads from 0–2 Nm.”


Problem discovered in Stage 5:

Lab tests show with 2 Nm load, speed error is ±15%.
Root cause: chosen MOSFET has higher Rds_on than expected; thermal issues.

Change Control Actions:

CR‑001 logged: propose changing MOSFET to a lower Rds_on device and updating PCB.
Impact analysis:

Cost + $10 per controller.
PCB re‑spin requires 2 extra weeks.
May affect EMI; new tests needed.

Decision: Approve with schedule adjustment; risk accepted.
Update:

CMP-SY-050 (power stage) design updated.
IMP-SY-105 (PCB files) new version tagged.
DCP‑300 and DCP‑400 updated.
Tests re‑run; new results linked to updated components.



This illustrates: - Real ECE constraints (thermal, power) causing design change. - How unmanaged changes would break traceability and schedule.

title: Stage 5
section: Technical Debt – The “Credit Card” of Engineering
text:
Technical Debt – The “Credit Card” of Engineering


Technical debt = extra work you incur later because you take shortcuts now.
Sources in ECE projects:

Quick‑and‑dirty code “just to make it work” with no tests.
Poorly labeled wires / undocumented pin mappings.
Temporary breadboard hacks instead of final PCB.
Incomplete calibration of sensors (“we’ll fix later”).
Skipping documentation (“we’ll write it at the end”).

Debt isn’t always bad if:

It’s intentional.
It’s documented.
You have a plan to pay it back.



















Note


Technical debt is like a credit card:
Used carefully, it helps.
Ignored, it compounds and crashes your project.





Ask students for examples from prior projects where they “hacked something” and then regretted it weeks later. Link technical debt to integration pain (things break when you combine subsystems).

title: Stage 5
text:
Managing Technical Debt – ECE Examples
Mixed‑Signal Sensor Front‑End

Shortcut:

Use a quick, unshielded breadboard layout for analog front‑end.

Debt:

Unpredictable noise; every test has random artifacts.


Managing:

Log debt item: “Replace breadboard with PCB (IMP-SY-201) before final validation.”
Reserve time in schedule and integrate into Stage 6 test plan.


Make them see that “we’ll just debug it later” is a decision that should be tracked like any other risk or change. Encourage them to keep a technical debt register alongside their risk register.

title: Stage 5
section: Integration Planning – Don’t Leave It to the Last Week
text:
Integration Planning – Don’t Leave It to the Last Week


Integration = combining components so they work as a coherent system.
Bad approach: - “We’ll each build our part and plug everything together the week before the demo.”
Better approach:

Plan integration in increments:

Start with critical paths (e.g., sensor → microcontroller → actuator).
Use stubs/mocks for parts not ready yet.

Align integration with verification plans:

Each integration step tied to specific REQ-* and VER-* tests.

Consider interfaces early:

Electrical levels, communication protocols, timing, data formats.














Integration steps example:

SENS ↔︎ CTRL (local link).
CTRL ↔︎ UI (basic local UI).
CTRL ↔︎ CLOUD (remote access).
Full system test with all four.



Point out that integration order matters. Encourage teams to identify high‑risk interfaces and integrate those first.

title: Stage 5
section: Integration Planning Example – Multi‑Board Power System
text:
Integration Planning Example – Multi‑Board Power System
Project: Programmable DC power supply with:

Control board (MCU + display).
Power board (buck/boost converter).
Communication interface (USB/CAN).

Integration Plan:

Stage 5 (early):

Integrate MCU with display only; verify UI flows.
Power board tested separately with dummy loads.

Stage 5 (mid):

Integrate controller board and power board:

Verify setpoint‑to‑output mapping.
Check ADC readings vs true voltage/current.


Stage 5/6 boundary:

Add USB/CAN link to PC app.
Execute partial system tests (logging, remote setpoint).

Stage 7:

Full integration (controller + power + comm + safety interlocks).
System verification against REQ-SY-* (accuracy, response time, safety).



Use this to show integration as a planned sequence, not a one‑shot event. Emphasize that each integration step should be accompanied by targeted tests.

title: Stage 5
text:
Tying Stage 5 Back to Traceability


At Stage 5, your traceability links focus on:

From components to implementation artifacts:

CMP-* → IMP-*

From implementation to upcoming tests:

IMP-* → TST-* (planned)


You should know for each IMP-*:

Which CMP-* it implements.
Which REQ-* ultimately depend on it.



















Important


If you can’t map an IMP-* artifact back to a CMP-* and REQ-*, ask: Why are we building this?





Remind them to keep the RTM updated as they implement and refactor. This ensures by Stage 6 they can design tests that cover all implemented requirements.

title: Stage 5
section: Minimal CM Setup – What Your Team Should Do This Week
text:
Minimal CM Setup – What Your Team Should Do This Week

Version Control:

Use a shared Git repo (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, self‑hosted).
Commit often with meaningful messages referencing IDs (e.g., REQ-SW-012).

Branching & Tagging:

main (or master) for stable, tested code.
dev and feature branches (e.g., feature-motor-driver).
Tag stable milestones (v0.1-prototype, v0.2-integration1).

Change Log & CRs:

Track major changes as issues or in a CR spreadsheet.
For each: link to requirements, components, commits.

Baselines:

Agree as a team what is Baseline 1 for DCP‑400 (e.g., “version used for first integrated test”).



You can show them a sample Git workflow diagram and/or a screenshot of a well‑structured repository. Offer guidelines for commit message format: [CMP-SW-050][REQ-SW-012] Implement basic PID loop

title: Stage 5
text:
Quick Interactive Exercise
You are working on an IoT‑enabled smart lighting system for a campus building.
In your team / with a neighbor:

List 3 configuration items (CIs) that should be under version control.
Propose one baseline you would like to establish in the next 3 weeks.
Describe one likely technical debt shortcut you might be tempted to take and how you would manage it.

We’ll share a few answers and discuss.

Walk around while they discuss; prompt them with hints: - CI could include firmware, PCB, cloud backend, mobile app, etc. - Baseline: first end‑to‑end demo version, first hardware revision, etc. - Shortcut: skipping proper security for the cloud, using global variables in firmware, etc.

title: Stage 5
text:
Summary – Stage 5 Takeaways

Configuration Management keeps your system reproducible and understandable.
Baselines are reference points that stabilize requirements, design, and implementation.
Change control prevents hidden, untracked changes from breaking your project.
Technical debt is inevitable; manage it consciously instead of ignoring it.
Integration planning should start early and proceed in planned increments, not last‑minute chaos.
ECE projects (FPGA DSPs, power converters, embedded controllers, IoT systems) need these practices just as much as large industrial systems.







Important


Stage 5 is where your project can either become a controlled engineering effort or descend into a fragile hack. Your CM and integration discipline determines which.




Close by connecting Stage 5 to upcoming Stage 6: Good CM and integration planning make unit testing and debugging easier. Poor CM multiplies effort in later stages. Encourage questions about repositories, tools, and how to structure DCP‑400.

title: Stage 5
text:
Next Steps for Your Team
Before the next lab:

Set up or review your Git (or other VC) workflow.
Identify and list your configuration items and proposed first baseline.
Create a simple change request template (issue template or spreadsheet).
Note at least two areas where you are carrying technical debt and how you plan to pay it down.

Bring this information to the lab – we’ll review and refine it together.

You can collect their minimal CM plans as a short graded or ungraded exercise. This forces them to concretely apply what they learned and sets better habits for the rest of the project.

title: Stage 5
text:
Stage 5 – Implementation & Configuration Management
Config Control, Baselines, Change Management, Technical Debt, Integration Planning
Capstone Design – Electrical & Computer Engineering

This session zooms in on Stage 5 of the capstone: Implementation. Students are likely already writing code and building hardware; now we teach them to manage that work professionally using configuration management, baselines, change control, and integration planning. Emphasize: lots of student capstones “die” here due to unmanaged changes and chaotic integration.

title: Stage 5
text:
Learning Objectives – Stage 5
By the end of this session, you will be able to:

Explain what configuration management (CM) means in a capstone project.
Define and use baselines (requirements, design, implementation) in your project.
Apply a simple change control process using change requests and approvals.
Recognize and manage technical debt in both HW and SW.
Plan for integration using a V‑Model mindset.
Relate these concepts to real ECE project examples (embedded systems, RF, power, FPGA, etc.).


These are behavior‑level objectives: students should do CM, not just know definitions. At the end, you can revisit these objectives and ask students to self‑assess where they feel strong/weak.

title: Stage 5
section: Where Are We in the V‑Model?
text:
Where Are We in the V‑Model?

















Note


Stage 5 sits at the bottom of the V:
You turn your designs (DCP‑300) into real artifacts (DCP‑400) while maintaining traceability.




Remind them: - Up to now: mostly documents, diagrams, and planning. - Now: code, hardware, prototypes – but you can’t abandon discipline. Stage 5 sets up the success of Stages 6–8 (testing & validation).

title: Stage 5
text:
Stage 5 in the Course Deliverables


Stage 5 Key Outcomes:

Implementation of planned components (CMP-* → IMP-*).
All work under configuration management (Git, etc.).
Defined implementation baseline at key milestones.
Active change control: every major divergence from design/requirements tracked.
Updated Requirements Traceability Matrix (RTM).


DCP‑400 – Implementation
Must include:

List of implementation artifacts (IMP-* IDs for code, schematics, PCBs, etc.).
Build scripts / README for building SW/HW.
Change log (approved changes vs original plan).
Updated baselines and CM strategy (branches/tags).



Stress: DCP‑400 is more than “we built it.” It documents how it was built, what versions are official, and how those versions relate back to requirements and design.

title: Stage 5
text:
Configuration Management – What & Why


Configuration Management (CM) is the discipline of:

Identifying the configuration items (CIs) that define your system.
Controlling changes to those items.
Keeping an accurate record of what version is used where.

Typical CIs in ECE Capstones:

Source code, HDL (VHDL/Verilog), firmware.
Schematics, PCB layouts, BOMs.
FPGA bitstreams, compiled binaries.
Test scripts, calibration data, FPGA constraints files.
Key documents (StRS, SyRS, design, test plans).



















Important


Without CM, you don’t know which version was tested or delivered.





Use the classic horror story:

Team tests version A.
Team demos accidentally with version B that was only half‑tested. They can’t reproduce results because they never tagged the working commit or documented differences.
